title: Sweet Sixteen

jokes.text: Oh, Ann, you beautiful, rule-breaking moth.
jokes.setup: Ann asks a simple yes/no question about campaign posters
jokes.punchline: Leslie responds with an absurdly poetic and overwrought description
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character writing - her tendency to be dramatically poetic about mundane things. 'Rule-breaking moth' is an inspired metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Do you want the campaign posters to say 'Vote Leslie Knope' or 'Leslie Knope for City Council'?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann, those posters aren't just about words on paper. They're a clarion call to the citizens of Pawnee, a rallying cry that echoes through the streets like a majestic eagle soaring over the Grand Canyon at sunset, reminding every man, woman, and child that there is hope, there is possibility, and there is Leslie Knope.

jokes.text: Boss, I need your Herbie Hancock on this. Outside.
jokes.setup: Ann needs Leslie's signature on something
jokes.punchline: Uses 'Herbie Hancock' as slang for signature, Leslie immediately shuts it down with 'Outside'
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay with the jazz musician's name. Leslie's immediate 'Outside' reaction is perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I need you to Herbie Hancock this for me.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Outside.

jokes.text: But it's great exercise... But it's a pain in the ass.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to find a positive spin on the campaign rules
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts herself, showing her frustration bleeding through her optimism
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's character trait of forced optimism breaking down. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Look, the campaign finance rules are actually a great opportunity for us to focus on grassroots organizing and authentic community engagement instead of spending money on ads and—you know what? This is insane. We're going to lose. We're going to lose so badly.

jokes.text: Parks is inside. Campaign is outside. Parks is inside. Campaign's outside.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to explain the rules to April as a 'fun game'
jokes.punchline: The repetitive, childlike explanation reveals how absurd the situation is
jokes.explanation: Leslie's earnest attempt to make bureaucracy fun is endearingly naive. The repetition adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay April, this is going to be so fun! We're going to play a game where you follow these rules. Rule one: you have to smile. Rule two: you have to say nice things. Rule three: you have to participate in team building activities.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Leslie, that's not a game, that's just... your life.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no, no! It IS a game because I made up rules! See? Rule four: if you break any of the other rules, you have to do the rules again, but this time while thinking about how fun it is!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're describing a psychological nightmare.

jokes.text: Ooh, you know what would make it more fun? - What? - Oh, my God, this.
jokes.setup: April pretending to be interested in Leslie's game
jokes.punchline: April's sarcastic enthusiasm followed by her hanging up
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment - her deadpan delivery and immediate hanging up is classic character behavior.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Oh wow, a game about municipal government? That sounds absolutely thrilling. I can't wait to spend my evening learning about zoning permits and budget allocations.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Right? I know, it's so much fun!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm gonna go ahead and hang up now.

jokes.text: Well, I have time to spare. I'm also volunteering at 'Wheels For Meals On Wheels.' We repair vans for 'Meals On Wheels.'
jokes.setup: Ron pointing out Leslie is overworked
jokes.punchline: Leslie's solution is to add MORE work, with an absurdly redundant organization name
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Leslie moment - her response to being overworked is to take on more work. The organization name is perfectly absurd bureaucratic speak.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, you're overworked. You need to delegate more.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're right, Ron. So I'm starting a new department: The Department of Delegation Oversight and Management. D.D.O.M. We'll meet every morning at 6 AM to discuss delegation strategies.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's the opposite of what I said.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know! Isn't it great?

jokes.text: Or I own four identical versions of the same sweater. No, you don't, there's an old lollipop that's been stuck to the back since Tuesday.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to defend wearing the same sweater four days
jokes.punchline: Ron's specific evidence that it's the same sweater
jokes.explanation: Great setup/payoff with Ron's deadpan delivery. The lollipop detail is perfectly gross and specific.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's not the same sweater! I have several identical sweaters.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: There's a Cheeto dust stain in the exact same spot on the shoulder. You've worn the same sweater four days in a row.

jokes.text: That's the style now, Ron. It's called 'lollipopping.' All the kids are doing it.
jokes.setup: Getting caught with a dirty sweater
jokes.punchline: Leslie's desperate attempt to make having candy stuck to her clothes sound trendy
jokes.explanation: Leslie's refusal to admit she's overwhelmed leads to this absurd improvisation about 'lollipopping' being trendy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no, no, it's not dirty, it's vintage. And these aren't stains, they're... they're artisanal candy accents. Very European. Very now.

jokes.text: I accept your apology, I will not take a sabbatical, as we agreed, and we will never discuss this again.
jokes.setup: Ron suggesting Leslie take a sabbatical
jokes.punchline: Leslie reframes the conversation as if Ron apologized and they agreed, when neither happened
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie denial mechanism - she completely rewrites reality to avoid admitting she needs help.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, I think you should take a sabbatical.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, Ron, I appreciate you saying that. And I'm glad we both agree that I'm indispensable to this office. Thank you for the apology.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I didn't apologize.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, Ron, Ron. We've both said some things. The important thing is we've worked through it and we're moving forward together.

jokes.text: Hold up, hold up, hold up. Hold up, hold up, wait. That's you.
jokes.setup: Tom gives Ann a gift 'for the most beautiful girl in Pawnee'
jokes.punchline: Tom's dramatic realization that Ann is standing right there
jokes.explanation: Tom's over-the-top romantic gesture followed by his comedic timing of 'realizing' Ann is there.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I got this gift for the most beautiful girl in Pawnee.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Tom, that's so sweet!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait... you're standing right here.

jokes.text: So I'm 'Tommy's girl,' and you're just 'Tom'? Why not 'Ann's man'? Nobody owns me, cupcake, not even you.
jokes.setup: Ann questioning the gendered nature of the matching hats
jokes.punchline: Tom's declaration of independence while calling her 'cupcake'
jokes.explanation: Tom's obliviousness to his own contradiction - claiming independence while using a possessive pet name.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Wait, why do we have to wear matching hats? That's so gendered.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ann, cupcake, I'm declaring my independence from hat-based gender norms.

jokes.text: We put the hats on-- Don't say it. We take everything else off.
jokes.setup: Ann cutting off Tom before he can suggest something sexual
jokes.punchline: Tom says it anyway in the most unsexy way possible
jokes.explanation: Typical Tom awkwardness. The joke works but is predictable sexual innuendo.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Tom, whatever you're about to say, don't.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I was just gonna say we should have intercourse.

jokes.text: These Kangol hats are exactly like the one Samuel L. Jackson wore to the Latin Grammys. How could she not like them?
jokes.setup: Tom's confusion about Ann not liking the hats
jokes.punchline: His weirdly specific reference to Samuel L. Jackson at the Latin Grammys
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom logic - his pop culture references are always oddly specific and he assumes everyone shares his enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ann doesn't like the hats? But I specifically designed them to be cool. They're like what Samuel L. Jackson would wear to the Latin Grammys if he went to the Latin Grammys, which he won't, because he's not Latin.

jokes.text: I really don't care what happens to them... Or anyone.
jokes.setup: April explaining why she's bad at giving relationship advice
jokes.punchline: Her casual admission that she doesn't care about anyone
jokes.explanation: Classic April deadpan honesty. The escalation from 'them' to 'anyone' is perfect character writing.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm not good at giving relationship advice because I don't actually care about anyone.

jokes.text: As the Germans would say, 'er ist ein wunder hund.' He is a wonder dog.
jokes.setup: Chris describing Champion
jokes.punchline: His unnecessarily German phrase followed by translation
jokes.explanation: Chris's over-enthusiasm and weird multilingual tendencies. The translation makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: His name is Champion. He's a very good boy. Sehr guter Junge.

jokes.text: The dog training course I took was conducted entirely in German, and so now I'm fluent in German... Words relating to dogs.
jokes.setup: Chris claiming to be fluent in German
jokes.punchline: The very specific limitation of his German knowledge
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff structure. Chris's over-enthusiasm deflated by the specificity of his actual knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm fluent in German.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Really? Say something in German.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Uh... Die Bratwurst ist sehr lecker.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's... that's just about sausage.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Yes. I am fluent in German sausage.

jokes.text: He's a mutt. Half amazing, half terrific.
jokes.setup: Describing Champion's breed
jokes.punchline: Chris's relentless positivity even in describing a mutt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chris moment - even when explaining mixed breed, he can only use positive descriptors.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Champion is a mixed breed. A lot of people might call him a mutt, but I prefer to think of him as a dog with a very diverse genetic portfolio. He's got a little bit of everything - German Shepherd, Labrador, some Collie maybe. It's like he's a living metaphor for the beautiful diversity of the canine world.

jokes.text: Ich bin ein three-legged dog.
jokes.setup: Chris showing off his German dog vocabulary
jokes.punchline: Mixing German with English in an absurd way
jokes.explanation: Callback to his German dog training. The mix of languages is absurd and very Chris.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I've been studying German, and I want to impress you with my knowledge of dog-related vocabulary.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: The German word for dog is 'hund.' A puppy is 'welpe.' And a really good boy is 'sehr guter hund.'

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: But here's where it gets interesting. If you combine the words, you get 'hundewelpeseHrguterhund,' which is technically one word in German.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: It means 'a dog puppy that is very good and also respects fitness routines and has perfect posture.'

jokes.text: Who wouldn't? That sounds like an amazing day, right?
jokes.setup: Chris describing Andy's lazy lifestyle negatively
jokes.punchline: Andy's genuine enthusiasm for the lifestyle Chris was criticizing
jokes.explanation: Andy's obliviousness to criticism is endearing. He genuinely thinks being lazy sounds amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You just sit around all day doing nothing, eating terrible food, not exercising, completely disregarding your health and responsibilities.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know! Isn't it great?

jokes.text: My birthday is February 29th, so I only get one real birthday, you know, once every four years.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why his birthday is hard to remember
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he was born on leap day
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the episode's premise. Jerry having a leap day birthday is both sad and perfect for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My birthday is February 29th. I was born on leap day.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So technically, I've only had twelve real birthdays.

jokes.text: We go, we split a huge piece of cotton candy cheesecake from Cakey J's Cheesecake Facility.
jokes.setup: Jerry describing his birthday celebration
jokes.punchline: The absurdly named 'Cakey J's Cheesecake Facility'
jokes.explanation: The name 'Cakey J's Cheesecake Facility' is wonderfully absurd - formal like a manufacturing plant for something so frivolous.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I went to this place called Cakey J's Cheesecake Facility for my birthday.

jokes.text: And then, well, we hold hands for a while. We listen to some Anita Baker. She meets up with some friends, and I turn in early.
jokes.setup: Jerry describing his wild birthday celebration
jokes.punchline: The increasingly mundane activities culminating in going to bed early
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating anticlimax. Each activity gets more mundane, ending with Jerry alone while his wife socializes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I had the most incredible birthday celebration. We started by going skydiving, then we went to this exclusive nightclub where I danced until 3 AM, then we went to this five-star restaurant and had the most amazing meal of my life, and then... we went to bed early.

jokes.text: 64 divided by 4 is-- 16. 64. So we're gonna throw him a Sweet 16 surprise party.
jokes.setup: Trying to figure out Jerry's leap day age
jokes.punchline: April's confident wrong answer followed by the absurd Sweet 16 party idea
jokes.explanation: April's deadpan wrong math followed by Leslie's enthusiastic embrace of the Sweet 16 concept for a 64-year-old is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So if Jerry was born on a leap day, he'd only have a birthday every four years. Let me think... if he's 61...

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: He's 15.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No, April, that's not even close.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We should throw him a Sweet 16 party. With a limo and those sashes.

jokes.text: Let's start a pool. Who wants retired? Who wants dead? Dead. No. I got one dead. No.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentioning Jerry might be retired or dead in four years
jokes.punchline: April immediately starting a betting pool on Jerry's death
jokes.explanation: Dark humor perfectly suited to April's character. Her immediate enthusiasm for betting on Jerry's death is shocking but in character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry might be retired or dead in four years.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm starting a betting pool on when Jerry dies.

jokes.text: We're not big on hospitality. The Meagles are a cold people.
jokes.setup: Donna reluctantly agreeing to let them use her lake house
jokes.punchline: Her matter-of-fact description of her family as cold
jokes.explanation: Donna's deadpan delivery and the formal phrasing 'The Meagles are a cold people' like she's describing an ancient tribe.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Fine. You can use my lake house. But I'm warning you, my family is cold. Not emotionally—we're very warm. I mean literally cold. We don't believe in heating. It's a waste of money and it builds character.

jokes.text: And if you listen closely, that is the bubbling of the hot tub. You do not have access to it.
jokes.setup: Donna describing the sounds of the house
jokes.punchline: Immediately denying access to the thing she just pointed out
jokes.explanation: Perfect structure - she draws attention to something nice then immediately forbids it. Very Donna to be simultaneously generous and withholding.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: That's the sound of the ice maker. And that's the sound of me denying you access to it.

jokes.text: Ginuwine's my cousin. Ginuwine? The Ginuwine is your cousin?
jokes.setup: Tom seeing Ginuwine memorabilia in Donna's house
jokes.punchline: The casual reveal that she's related to a famous R&B star
jokes.explanation: Good reveal moment. Donna's casual attitude toward having a famous cousin fits her mysterious background.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, is that... Ginuwine memorabilia? Why do you have all this?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: He's my cousin.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Your cousin? Ginuwine is your cousin?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Yeah. We don't really talk about it.

jokes.text: Ginuwine is Ginuwine. He's Ginuwine.
jokes.setup: Andy not knowing who Ginuwine is
jokes.punchline: Tom's increasingly frustrated attempts to explain using just the name
jokes.explanation: Tom's inability to explain celebrity culture to Andy. The repetition shows his frustration but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ginuwine. The R&B singer. He's only one of the greatest artists of all time.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I don't know who that is.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ginuwine. Ginuwine the Pony Boy. He sings 'Pony.' The song 'Pony.'

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Still not ringing a bell.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: How do you not know Ginuwine?! Ginuwine! GI-NU-WINE!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Is he a food?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No! He's Ginuwine! The artist! Ginuwine!

jokes.text: Not loving '90s R&B music is number three on the 'oh-no-nos' list.
jokes.setup: Tom's relationship dealbreakers
jokes.punchline: Having very specific pop culture requirements for dating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom character moment - his shallow priorities and the term 'oh-no-nos' is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Look, I have some dealbreakers. You need to have seen all of The Wire. You need to understand the cultural significance of the original Ghostbusters. And you need to know that the second Die Hard movie isn't a Christmas movie.

jokes.text: Toxic. Merkel. - Merkel. - What is 'Merkel'? She's the Chancellor of Germany.
jokes.setup: Chris giving German dog commands
jokes.punchline: Using the German Chancellor's name as a dog command
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment - using a world leader's name as a dog command shows how confused Chris's German vocabulary is.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Merkel!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Did you just use the German Chancellor's name as a dog command?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Well, yes. In Germany, we use 'Merkel' to—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's incredible.

jokes.text: Crow for dinner tonight, Mr. Swanson?
jokes.setup: Leslie proving she can multitask successfully
jokes.punchline: Her premature victory lap with the 'eating crow' reference
jokes.explanation: Leslie's overconfidence about to be undercut. The idiom reference is clever but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm multitasking like a boss! I can handle the budget report AND eat this sandwich at the same time.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: See? Nailed it. I'm basically a superhero.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, you just dropped the entire sandwich on the budget report.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well... I guess I'm eating crow now. Literally, if this is what I think it is.

jokes.text: Guys, someone was supposed to invite Jerry. It's his surp-- Crap, it was me. I forgot to invite Jerry.
jokes.setup: Leslie running a perfect surprise party
jokes.punchline: She forgot the most basic element - inviting the guest of honor
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic payoff to Leslie's overconfidence. The structure of her realizing it was her job is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I planned the perfect surprise party! The decorations are amazing, the food is catered by the best restaurant in town, the DJ is playing all the right songs, and—

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, where's Tom?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What do you mean where's Tom? He's... he's...

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Did you invite him?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I... I was so focused on making everything perfect that I forgot to actually invite him.

jokes.text: By the end of the night, you are going to take a bite of Jerry's cake, which incidentally, we need to pick up on the way home, and you are gonna say, 'sabbatical, schmabbatical. Leslie can do it all. She's the best, and I'm stupid.'
jokes.setup: Leslie defending her ability to multitask after forgetting Jerry
jokes.punchline: Her increasingly specific and self-aggrandizing prediction
jokes.explanation: Leslie's delusional confidence reaching new heights. The parenthetical about needing to pick up cake shows she's still forgetting things.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can multitask. I'm doing it right now. I'm listening to you, and I'm also mentally preparing my acceptance speech for when I'm named Indiana's first female governor.

jokes.text: Ann, I'm at 600. Are you really not stopping me? I have those cotton t-shirt sheets. Huh?
jokes.setup: Tom testing Ann's sheet thread count standards
jokes.punchline: Ann's admission that she has the cheapest possible sheets
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Tom and Ann's lifestyle incompatibility. Tom's horror at cotton sheets fits his materialistic character.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So these sheets are 1000 thread count?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yes, that's the minimum for quality sheets.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And you're telling me these are the cheapest sheets you could find?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Well, I... yes. These are literally the cheapest sheets at Target.

jokes.text: She's never seen a single Paul Walker movie? That's a huge 'oh-no-no.' She also 'doesn't care' about Blu-ray? She's a monster.
jokes.setup: Tom listing Ann's cultural failings
jokes.punchline: Calling her a 'monster' over technology preferences
jokes.explanation: Tom's priorities are hilariously shallow. The escalation to 'monster' over Blu-ray is appropriately ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ann, you don't have a smartphone, you don't use email, you refuse to go on social media... You're a monster.

jokes.text: I own more pairs of Uggs than she does.
jokes.setup: Tom listing relationship incompatibilities
jokes.punchline: A man owning more women's boots than his girlfriend
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom moment - his metrosexual tendencies taken to absurd extremes. Great economy in the delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: She likes to go hiking, I like to stay indoors and online shop for women's boots. She's a morning person, I'm a night owl. She wants to have kids, I want to have more pairs of women's boots than my girlfriend.

jokes.text: I was getting kind of sick of listening to Tom and Ann talk about their relationship, but then I remembered that alcohol existed. Thank you, alcohol.
jokes.setup: April being annoyed by Tom and Ann's relationship drama
jokes.punchline: Her discovery of alcohol as a solution
jokes.explanation: Perfect April solution to social discomfort. Her grateful tone toward alcohol is perfectly deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Tom and Ann are fighting again. Something about his restaurant idea and her job. I don't know. I stopped listening after 'We need to talk.' That's when I discovered this bottle of wine behind my desk that I don't remember buying. Best discovery since I found out how to block people on Facebook.

jokes.text: Well, because I thought you were Gayle. What are you doing in my bathroom?
jokes.setup: Jerry calling Leslie and Ron into his house
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he's naked in the bathtub and confused
jokes.explanation: Good cringe setup for the awkward situation. Jerry's confusion adds to the discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Leslie, Ron, come in here!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, what's going on?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Gail, we're coming in.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm in here! In the bathroom!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: What in...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? What's the problem? I'm just taking a bath.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, we can see you. You're... you're completely naked.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well yes, Leslie, that's how one takes a bath.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Why did you call us in here?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I didn't call you in here. You called me and said you were coming over. So I was getting ready.

jokes.text: Oh, did we lose more seniors in the woods? No, it's a sinkhole, right?
jokes.setup: Jerry trying to guess the Parks emergency
jokes.punchline: His casual assumption that they regularly lose seniors in the woods
jokes.explanation: Brilliant throwaway line suggesting this is a recurring problem in Pawnee. Jerry's casual tone makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Did you lose a senior in the woods again?

jokes.text: Sometimes you just have to let a dog be a dog. He'll be all right. Champion! He'll come back.
jokes.setup: Andy confidently defending letting Champion off leash
jokes.punchline: Champion immediately running away
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic timing. Andy's confident philosophy immediately backfires, which is very Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: He's a good boy. He knows to stay close.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: See? Total obedience. Champion, come!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Champion? Champion!

jokes.text: You don't need to read the whole thing. 9u/09230023-- 099twposter-- it does say that. .jpeg. Jpeg. Yes, Walter, it does say that.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to explain the sign printing error
jokes.punchline: Walter methodically reading every character of the URL
jokes.explanation: Good escalating frustration moment. The bureaucratic stubbornness vs Leslie's impatience creates tension.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So the sign company just printed whatever was in the email without checking it.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Let me read this back to you: W-W-W dot parks and rec dot gov slash events slash harvest festival underscore two thousand twelve.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes, that's the website—

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Hold on. There's more. Backslash admin backslash uploads backslash documents backslash.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Walter, you don't need to read every single—

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I'm almost done. P-D-F question mark session equals one-seven-three-four-five-six.

jokes.text: You put 20" rims on your Volkswagen Golf, and you insist on being introduced as 'the brown Gosling.'
jokes.setup: Ann listing her 'oh-no-nos' about Tom
jokes.punchline: Tom's absurd car modifications and self-comparison to Ryan Gosling
jokes.explanation: Excellent character specificity. Both details are perfectly Tom - the impractical car mods and delusional celebrity comparison.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Tom, you can't just modify your car like this. It's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ridiculous? Ann, I've added racing stripes, a spoiler, and underglow lighting. I'm basically Ryan Gosling now.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Ryan Gosling doesn't drive around with a neon sign that says 'Treat Yo' Self' on the back.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's because he hasn't thought of it yet. When he does, he'll wish he was me.

jokes.text: Everything you just said makes me like me more.
jokes.setup: Ann listing Tom's annoying behaviors
jokes.punchline: Tom taking pride in the things that annoy her
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom response - his narcissism is so complete that criticism becomes validation. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You're always trying to get out of work, you're constantly making everything about money, and you never listen to what anyone else is saying.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Thank you. I've been working on those things.

jokes.text: We didn't get to the drug store for my liver medication. Well, you should've thought about that before we dragged you out of the bath, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Jerry mentioning they forgot his medication
jokes.punchline: Leslie blaming Jerry for not anticipating being kidnapped from his bath
jokes.explanation: Leslie's inability to admit fault leads to blaming Jerry for not being prepared for kidnapping. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They forgot my medication.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, Jerry, you should have anticipated being kidnapped from your bath and planned accordingly.

jokes.text: Oh, what the [Bleep]? Surprise. Where the hell is everybody?
jokes.setup: Big surprise party reveal
jokes.punchline: Empty house because it's too late and Champion ran away
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to Leslie's incompetence. Jerry's confusion and profanity add to the awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Surprise!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh no, where is everyone? It's midnight.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And Champion's gone. He ran away.

jokes.text: Champion, you're lost and soon you'll be found / I rescued you from the pound, something something oh man on the ground / get on out of here
jokes.setup: Andy singing to call Champion back
jokes.punchline: His terrible improvised lyrics falling apart mid-song
jokes.explanation: Andy's musical incompetence is endearing. The progression from decent to nonsensical lyrics is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Champion! Come back, boy! Champion, champion, champion... you're the best dog in the... in the... *voice cracks* ...I don't know what rhymes with champion.

jokes.text: Dog whistle.
jokes.setup: Andy amazed that Champion came to his voice
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Chris used a dog whistle
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Andy's moment. Great economy - just two words that recontextualize everything.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, Champion came to my voice?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Actually, I've been using this dog whistle the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A dog whistle? Chris!

jokes.text: It's also about Ann and I getting back together and being the hottest couple in Pawnee. I'd also like to announce our official relationship mash-up name. It's 'Tan.' To Tan.
jokes.setup: Tom giving a toast at Jerry's party
jokes.punchline: Making Jerry's birthday about his own relationship and announcing a couple name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom narcissism - making someone else's celebration about himself. The mash-up name is appropriately cringey.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Jerry, you've been such a good friend to me over the years. And tonight, I want to celebrate you by talking about something that's really important to me: my relationship with Lucy. We've decided on a couple name. We're going to be Tomcy. It's Tom and Lucy combined. Tomcy.

jokes.text: I would like to announce that the name Tan is officially over. To the end of Tan.
jokes.setup: Tom proudly announcing their couple name
jokes.punchline: Ann immediately killing it
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate callback and deflation. Ann's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I've been working on our couple name. Are you ready? Tann. T-Ann.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's the worst thing I've ever heard.

jokes.text: I would like to address the fact that rule number seven says, 'no pets,' and yet there is a three-legged animal in my living room.
jokes.setup: Donna giving a toast at the party
jokes.punchline: Treating the toast as a formal complaint about house rules
jokes.explanation: Perfect Donna - treating a birthday toast as a formal complaint. The clinical description of Champion is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I'd like to propose a toast. First of all, no shoes on the furniture. Second, all dishes must be washed immediately after use. Third, quiet hours are from 10 PM to 8 AM. Fourth, if you're going to use the last of the milk, you replace it. And finally, under no circumstances are you to touch my things in the refrigerator.

jokes.text: Jerry's work is often adequate.
jokes.setup: Ron giving his Jerry toast
jokes.punchline: The most begrudging compliment possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson - even at a birthday party, his praise is minimal and bureaucratic. 'Often adequate' is hilariously faint praise.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Jerry, you're a good man. And I say that with the utmost sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You show up to work every day, you do your job, and you don't complain. Well, you do complain, but not as much as you could.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: So, Jerry... you're not the worst.

jokes.text: I have a stepbrother who lives in London and 2.8% body fat.
jokes.setup: Chris giving a toast about having things to be thankful for
jokes.punchline: His random personal facts that have nothing to do with anything
jokes.explanation: Very Chris - turning a moment about others into fitness and personal stats. The randomness is in character.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I have so much to be thankful for. My health, my friends, my family. But I'm also grateful for some of the smaller things in life. Like the fact that I can touch my nose with my tongue. And that I've never broken a bone. And that I can name all the US state capitals in alphabetical order.

jokes.text: You probably should just sit there and not move. She's really tired. Just a few hours. Few hours?
jokes.setup: April falling asleep on Andy
jokes.punchline: Andy's horror at being trapped for hours
jokes.explanation: Standard sitcom situation but Andy's panic is endearing. Not particularly original but fits the characters.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh no, oh no, oh no. April's asleep on me. If I move, she'll wake up. If I don't move, I'll be stuck here for hours.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Just enjoy the moment, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Easy for you to say! You're not the one slowly losing feeling in your arm!

jokes.text: I used to work in a sheet metal factory, but then a job came along at the tannery. The hours were better, and I would get paid. Also I'd have the chance to work with leather both before and after it was on the cow, which had always been a dream of mine.
jokes.setup: Ron telling a story to make a point about focus
jokes.punchline: His dream of working with cow leather before and after it was on the cow
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron backstory - the matter-of-fact delivery of an absurd childhood dream. 'Before and after it was on the cow' is perfectly weird.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have a dream of working with cow leather before and after it was on the cow.

jokes.text: How old were you? 11.
jokes.setup: Ron describing working multiple jobs and finishing middle school
jokes.punchline: The reveal he was only 11 years old
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup/payoff. Ron's matter-of-fact delivery makes the absurd age reveal even funnier. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I had three jobs by the time I was in middle school. I was working at a sawmill, I had a small firearms manufacturing operation in my garage, and I was doing carpentry on the side.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's incredible. How old were you?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Eleven.

jokes.text: Never half-ass two things. Whole-ass one thing.
jokes.setup: Ron's life lesson from his childhood story
jokes.punchline: His crude but profound philosophy
jokes.explanation: Instant classic Ron Swanson quote. Perfect blend of crude language and genuine wisdom. Became hugely quotable.

jokes.text: Yes, but I hate them. I know you do, sweetheart.
jokes.setup: Andy saying people want to talk to April because she's important to them
jokes.punchline: April's honest admission she hates them and Andy's accepting response
jokes.explanation: Sweet moment showing how well Andy understands April. His acceptance of her antisocial nature is touching and funny.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I hate them.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know. And that's what makes you so great.

jokes.text: Amazingly, yes. Muncie, Indiana.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking if they're sending him to Muncie for his getaway
jokes.punchline: Leslie's amazement that he correctly guessed such an unglamorous destination
jokes.explanation: Good Jerry moment - his dream destination is hilariously modest. Leslie's 'amazingly' shows she knows how sad it is.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, are you sending me to Muncie?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: How did you... I mean, yes. How did you know that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Lucky guess.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I am genuinely shocked that you correctly identified the least glamorous destination in Indiana on your first try.

tmdb_overview: Leslie realizes that the department has forgotten Jerry's birthday and decides to throw a party for him at a special location. Meanwhile, Tom is flabbergasted when he finds out Ann doesn't share his taste in music.
insight: Steady character work drives a mid-tier ep; 58 jokes land despite modest 75.6 score.
